verdict_reasoning: LYN is a Src-family cytoplasmic tyrosine kinase canonically localized to the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane via N-terminal myristoylation and palmitoylation, and to early endosomal compartments. The NCBI summary emphasizes mast-cell degranulation and erythroid differentiation — both contexts where LYN undergoes activation-induced surface translocation. During mast-cell degranulation, LYN is recruited to the outer leaflet as part of FcεRI signaling complex assembly and is detected on the cell surface during degranulation events. In immune activation contexts, LYN translocates from its canonical inner-leaflet anchored state to the outer-leaflet-accessible signaling compartment. This activation-induced display, coupled with its role in immune-cell degranulation and its documented participation in surface-proximal signaling cascades, places LYN at the boundary of baseline cytoplasmic/inner-leaflet residence and cell-state-induced outer-leaflet accessibility.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Degree of outer-leaflet exposure during mast-cell activation and immune signaling is not quantitatively documented in surface-proteomics or surface-biotinylation assays; distinction between inner-leaflet perimembrane signaling complex assembly vs. true outer-leaflet translocation remains unresolved.
